PREFACE TO FIFTH EDITION .
The firsteditionoftheseCodes was preparedand issuedby me
in 1888,and sincethattime,at intervalsof aboutsixyears,new
editionshave been issued,the presentbeingthe fifthone.
Thiseditionhas beenentirelyprintedfrom new plates,and con-
tainsallamendments to date, and the annotationsincludeall
casesreportedin 150 Kentucky, bringingthe notesof decisions
down to December,1912. There has alsobeen added tothenotes
citationsof the South Western Reporter,so thatthecitationsin-
clude the Kentucky Law Reporter, the South Western Reporter
and the Kentucky Reports.
The Kentucky Law Reporter,in which allopinionsof the court
were published eitherin fullor in abstract, was firstissued in
July,1880,and continued untilMarch ,1908,when itspublication
was suspended. During this time there were issued thirty-two
volumes- volume thirty-three was commenced and a few cases
published in it, but as no index has ever been prepared to this
volume the publicationof the Reporter may be said to have ended
with volume thirty-two .
The South Western Reporter, in which allthe opinions of the
courtare published,was firstissuedin August,1886- beginning
with volume eight of the Law Reporter and volume eighty-four
of the Kentucky Reports and itspublicationhas continued to
the present time.
Priorto September,1910— when theweeklypublicationby the
State of the Advance Sheets of the Kentucky Reports was be-
gun- the Kentucky Reports do not contain all opinions of the
court, only such casesbeingpublishedin theKentucky Reports
as the courtdirected,but beginningwith 140 Kentucky Reports,
in September, 1910, allopinionsof the court are publishedin
fullin the Kentucky Reports and in the Advance Sheets of the
Kentucky Reports .
In theAppendix willbe found sectionsof theKentucky Stat-
utes relatingto subjectsthatare oftenused in connectionwith
the Code .
NEW CASTLE ,KY .,January ,1913.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
595991

PREFACE TO FOURTH EDITION .
The Codes of Practiceeditedby me have met with a generous
receptionfrom the professionand I have endeavored to make
some substantialimprovement in each edition.
This editionhas been revised, and printed from new platesand
contains allamendments and acts relatingto the Code including
thosepassed by the General Assembly of 1906.
The annotations include all cases in the first twelve numbers of
28 Kentucky Law Reporter,-cited as "R ",-bringing notesdown
to March 15,1906.
When a case ispublished in the Ky .Reports and the Ky . Law
Reporter, both publicationsare cited. The Law Reporter begins
with 78 Ky .and 116 Ky .isthe lastReport issued,since itsissual
the Law Reporter alone is cited.
In the Appendix willbe found the rulesof the Court of Appeals
and the sectionsof the Kentucky Statutes relating:
1. To the jurisdictionof the courts of the State.
2. To courtshaving sixjudges.
3. To courts having continuous sessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminal cases.
NEW CASTLE , KY ., June , 1906 .
JOHN D. CARROLL .

PREFACE TO THIRD EDITION .
This edition contains all amendments and notes of decisions to
July 1,1900,includingcasesreportedin 101 Ky .and 21 Ky .Law
Reporter. The book has been entirelyreprintedand revised, and
allnotes willbe found in appropriateplaces. The number of the
sectionson each page are placed at top of page, and other im-
provements have been made in the arrangement .
In the Appendix will be found the rules of the Court of Appeals
and the sectionsof the Kentucky Statutesrelating:
1. To the jurisdictionof the courts of the State.
2. To courtshaving four judges.
3. To courtshaving continuous sessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminalcases.
Also the laws of the United States concerning removal of ac-
tionsand prosecutionsto the Federal courts.
NEW CASTLE , KY ., Sept., 1900.
JOHN D. CARROLL .

PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION .
This editionof the Codes containsallamendments ,and notesof
decisionsto January, 1895, includingcasesreportedin 95 Ky .
and 16 Ky . Law Reporter. Unreported decisionsof the Court of
Appeals found in theKy .Law Reportersbearingupon sectionsof
the Codes have also been inserted .
An index in the middle of a book seems out of place,and I have
put indexesto both Codes, as well as forms, at the end of the
volume .
The catch words to sections, the indexes and the notes have been
revised, rewrittenand made more complete, and many forms not
in former editions have been added .
In an appendix willbe found the sectionsof the Kentucky Stat-
utes relating:
1. To the jurisdictionof allcourts in the State.
2. To courtshaving four judges.
3. To courts having continuous sessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminal cases.
Also the laws of the United States relating to the Removal of
Actions and Prosecutions to the United States Courts, and the
Rules adopted by the Court of Appeals of Kentucky .
In the citation of cases I have not assumed to criticise or com-
ment , nor undertaken to say what the Court of Appeals willde-
cide when it comes to construe sectionsnot heretofore passed
upon , but have contented myself with statingthe law as I have
found itin the cases. My observationhas been that the court does
not always adopt the views of the commentator , and that expres-
sionsof opinion are more misleading than useful.
The Kentucky Statutescontains the general laws of the State
not found in the Codes, and the relationbetween the Statutes
and Codes isso intimate that I have in every instance where either
a section of the Codes or a note thereto contained any reference to
the Statutes, or any sectionof the Statutes had any bearing on
the Codes,citedand notedthesectionof the Statutesapplicable.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
NEW CASTLE , KY ., March ,1895.

PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION .
The originalCode of CivilPracticewas adopted by the Legisla-
ture in March , 1851 ; thisCode was revisedand amended ,a Crim-
inal Code prepared, and , in 1854, the Codes of Practice in civil
and criminal cases were adopted .
The presentCodes were prepared, under an actof the Legisla-
ture, by Richard A. Buckner and Joshua F. Bullitt, Commis-
sioners, and Alvin Duvall,umpire;were adoptedby the Legisla-
ture in 1876,and took effectJanuary 1,1877.
This editioncontainsallamendments to date,and a number of
actsrelatingdirectlyto the subject-matter of the Codes . I have
added at the end of the Civil Code an appendix giving original
and appellatejurisdictionof courts in civilcases; and article2,
chapter 66 , General Statutes relating to rent- mode and manner
of recovery .
The notesof decisionsof theCourt ofAppeals have,as a rule,
been confined to cases decided since 1851 - but a number of notes
of decisions prior to that date have been inserted- and include
cases reportedin 84 Ky ., bringing notes down to January
term ,1887.
The sectionsin brackets referto corresponding sectionsin the
Codes of 1854 ;following the index of the Criminal Code is a table
showing corresponding sectionsof that Code and Code of 1854.
I trustthat the notes, referencesand forms willbe found com-
plete,the arrangement convenient, and that, as a whole,the book
will meet with favor and prove usefulto the profession.
I desireto acknowledgemy deep obligationsto Judge Joseph
Barbour forvaluablesuggestionsand advicefrequentlyaskedfor,
and cheerfullygiven. To E. W. Hines, Esq ., I am indebted for
his courtesyin furnishingme advance pages of 84 Ky .
JOHN D. CARROLL .
NEW CASTLE , KY ., September , 1888

THE
CIVIL CODE OF PRACTICE .
TITLE I.
PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS .
§ 1. Civil cases defined.
§ 2. Civil actions defined .
§ 3. Specialproceedings.
§ 1. [Civilcasesdefined.] Civilcasesare actionsor specialpro-
ceedings .
§ 2. [Civilactionsdefined.] A civilactionisa demand ,by plead-
ings,in a courtofjustice,fortheenforcementof an allegedright
of a plaintiffagainsta defendant. ("Action" further defined,
sec. 732-34 .)
§ 3. [Specialproceedings.] Every othercivilcaseisa specialpro-
ceeding .
§ 2. (1) Civil actions. See note 1 to
sec . 3 .
(2) Provisional remedy , such as an at-
tachment or injunction,isnot a causeof
action, it is incidental to the action in
which it issought, and can only be ob-
tained in the court in which the action
was brought or ispendingat the time it
is sought. Moore v. Sheppard, 1 Met .
97 .
§ 3. (1) Special proceedings. The
word "action" embraces specialproceed-
ings. Secs. 732-34. In the absence of
any specialauthority for a summary
remedy thepartymust resortto a civil
actionfor the enforcement and protec-
tion of his privaterights. Gay v.Mor-
gan,4 Bush 606, and it is only in cases
where a specificremedy is providedby
statute that rights can be enforced
otherwisethan by civilaction. Stephens
v .Miller, 80 Ky . 47, 3 R. 523 ; Kinney v .
O'Bannon , 6 Bush 692 ; Combs v . Com .,
24 R. 1310, 71 S. W. 504. For some of
the casesinwhich summary proceedings
may be resorted to, see sec. 444. Veach
v . Wickersham , 11 Bush 261 .
(2) Claimants' bond for propertylev-
ied on under execution- motion for judg-
ment on, as provided in sec. 449, is a
specialor summary proceeding. Couch-
man v.Maupin, 78 Ky . 33; Watson v.
Gabby , 18 B. М. 658.
(3) Bastardy case is a civilor special
proceeding. Francis v . Com ., 3 Bush 4 ;
Chandler v. Com ., 4 Met . 66 ; Runner v .
Com ., 78 Ky . 556, 1 R. 257.
(4 ) Proceeding to enforce a forfeited
recognizanceor bond is a civilproceed-
ing. Com . v . Hughes , 13 Bush 349 .

§ 6 [4,5] [Action- when to be equitable; when ordinary.] Unless
otherwise provided by thisCode or other statute-
1. [Equitable actions.] Actions of which courts of chancery
had jurisdictionbefore the firstday of August , 1851, may be
equitable;and actionsof which such jurisdictionwas exclusive
must be equitable.
2. [Ordinaryactions.] Allotheractionsmust be ordinary.
§ 4. Forms, not substance, abolished
by Code; and the pleadermust statein
ordinary and conciselanguage the facts
constitutingthe cause of action. Every
fact necessaryto enablethe plaintiffto
recovermust be alleged, and every es-
sentialallegationrequiredto make a
declarationgood at common law upondemurrer must be made . The facts
must be allegedso as to enablethe op-
positeparty to know what ismeant to
be proved, and also that an issuemay
be formed in regardto the subject-mat-
ter in disputeand to enablethe courtto
pronounce the law upon the factsstated.
LouisvilleCanal Co. v. Murphy , 9 Bush
522; Stiversv.Baker,87 Ky .508,10R.
523, 9 S. W. 491; Murphy v. Estes, 6
Bush 532; Huffaker v. Nat . Bank , 12
Bush 287 .
§ 6. (1) Distinction between courts
of equityand courtsof law has not been
abolishedby the Code, but ifan action
be broughtinequitywhen itshouldhave
been in ordinary, or viceversa, it shall
not on that account be dismissed, but
transferredto the properdocket. Fraley
v . Peters, 12 Bush 469 ; and see sec.8 .
(2) Equitable and ordinary actions
defined. The pleadings in equitable and
ordinary actionsare the same , except
that a petitionin equity must be de-
scribedby thewords "petitionin equity."
Secs.110-2.Without attemptingto state
fullywhat actionsshould be equitable
and what ordinary,itmay be statedthat
equityhas jurisdictionof actionson re-
turn of "no property" and fordiscovery,
sec.439; by suretyagainstprincipalbe-
fore debt matures, sec. 237, and after
maturityof debt, sec.661; forsaleof
realpropertyof infantsand personsof
unsound mind, sec. 489, and of joint
owners, sec.490; to settletrustestates
and estatesof deceased persons, sec.428 ;
to grant divorcesand for alimony, sec.
420; to grant injunction, sec. 271; to
partitionland and allotdower,sec.499;

Title II] [§ 7-8Form of Civil Actions .
§ 7 [6] [Lost or mutilatedwriting- actionon- when bond to be
executed.] An ordinaryorequitableactionmay be broughtupon a
billof exchange,or a note or otherobligation,or upon an indorse-
ment or assignment thereof,which ,or upon a judgment the record
of which is lost, destroyed, mutilated, or defaced, without fraud
on the part of the plaintiff, or of those under whom he claims.
But no action shallbe brought upon an instrument transferable
by delivery,merely,which isallegedto be lost,destroyed, muti-
lated, or defaced, without a previous tender by the plaintiffto
the defendant, ifhis name and place of residencebe known to the
plaintiff, of an indemnifying bond , with good surety; nor shall
judgment be given against the defendant, in such action, until
such bond is given, with good surety approved by the court.
(Form of bond ,page 698.)
§ 8 [7] [Erroras toformofactionmay be corrected.] An errorof
the plaintiffas to the form of actionshallbe cause, not for the
abatement or dismissalof it, but merely for a change intothe
proper proceedingsby an amendment of the pleadingsand a
transferof the actionto the proper docket.

maintain a common law action for dam-

§ 8. (1) Error as to form of action-
how corrected. When the courthas ju-
risdiction, an error as to the form of
action adopted does not authorize a dis-
missal; it should be transferredto the
proper docket. Turner v. Newman , 19
R. 231, 39 S. W. 504; Landsdale v .
Mitchell, 14 B. M. 348; Foster v. Wat-
son, 16 B. M. 377 ; 15 B. M. 168 ; 1 Met .
593; 12 Bush.469. And advantage must
be taken of the error by a motion to
transferto proper docket. Greenup Co.
v. M. & B. S. R. Co.,88 Ky . 659,11 R.
169, 11 S. W. 774 .
(2) Practice. In an ordinaryactionto
recoverpossessionof property,a liencan
not be enforced without an amendment
asking that reliefand a transfer to
equity. Herrman v. Whitescarver, 89
Ky . 633, 11 R. 786, 13 S. W. 103; and
see Walker v. Leslie, 90 Ky . 642, 12 R.
581, 14 S. W. 682, and secs. 10, 11, and
notes thereto .

§ 9-10] [Title IIForm of Civil Actions .
§ 9 [8] [Error- how corrected.] The error mentioned in the last
sectionmay be correctedby the plaintiff,without motion , at any
time beforethe defendant answers,or afterward by leaveof court.
§ 10 [9] 1. [Equitableaction,transferof toordinary- transfergen-
erally.] The defendant, by motion made when he answers, may
have an equitableactiontransferredto the ordinarydocket, if,
according to the provisionsof section6, it should have been an
ordinary action, and ifthe answer present a defense of which he
isentitledto a jury trial.
2. [Motion of defendant to transferbefore or when he an-
swers.] The defendant, by motion made when or beforehe an-
swers,may have an ordinary action transferredto the equity
docket, if, accordingto the provisionsof section6, itshould
have been an equitableaction. (Depositionsmay be read,sec.
552.)
3. [Court may transferor try.] The courtmay ,withoutmo-
tion,order the transferof an actionfrom one docket to another
if either party be entitledto such transfer; or may try the
action, or renderjudgment thereinby default, unlessa party
entitled to a transfer move therefor .
4. [Transfer of complicated action to equity.] [The court
may ,in itsdiscretion,on motion ofeitherparty,or withoutmo-
tion, order the transferof an actionfrom the ordinaryto the
equitydocket,or from a court of purely common law to a court
of purelyequityjurisdiction,whenever the courtbeforewhich
the actionispending shallbe of the opinion that such transfer
isnecessarybecauseof the peculiarquestionsinvolved,or be-
cause the caseinvolvesaccountsso complicatedor such great
detailof factsas to render itimpracticablefor a jury to intel-
ligentlytry the case.] (Words in bracketsinsertedby act of
1890.)
§ 9. Motion to transferto ordinaryis
equivalentto an objectionto a trialby
the chancellor, and if motion is im-
properlyoverruledit is a reversibleer-
ror. Walker v. Leslie,90 Ky .642, 12 R.
581,14 S. W. 682.

§ 13. [Transferofactionsfromonecourttoanother.] In counties
wherein the jurisdictionof circuitcourts is vested in different
courts-
1. [Applicationof this chapter.] The provisionsof this
chapter, concerning transfersto the proper docket, apply to
transfers to the proper court .
2. [Transferfrom one court to another- practice.] Upon an
order for the transferof an action from one court to another,
the papers in the action and a copy of the order shallbe deliv-
ered by the clerk of the court which makes the order to the
clerk of the court to which the action is transferred ; and it
shall proceed as if it had been brought in the lattercourt.
(Transfer of attachments,sec.210. See as to transferin courts,
having 7 judges,page 652.)
§ 14 [11] [Bond may be requiredbeforeordinaryactiontransferred.]

affidavitthat he verilybelieveshe willsucceed in the action, and

gered by delay arisingfrom such transfer;and ifsuch affidavitbe
filed, the court shall not grant the motion of the defendant to
transfer, except upon condition that the defendant execute a
bond , with good surety, approved by the court, that he will pay
any judgment rendered in favor of the plaintiff: Provided,
That no such bond shallbe required if the trialof the case or
issue transferred take place during the term at which the transfer
ismade ,nor untilallissuesnot transferredare triedor disposed
of in favorof the plaintiff. (Form of affidavit, page 700. Form
of bond ,page 697.)

not assignable

rie,4 Met . 147.

§ 19 [31] [Assigneeof chose in action, defensesallowed against-

count, set-offor defensenow allowed; and ifthe assignmentbe
not authorizedby statutethe assignormust be a party, as plain-

quired to secure costs, sec. 619 ; see further as to assignablein-

§ 19. (1) Assignmentsauthorizedby

§ 20. (1) Assignment pending suit-
costs. Where a rightof actionis as-
signed, pending the suit, and the as-
signeesubstitutedon the recordwithout
objectionfrom the adverse party, the
originalparty is releasedfrom liability
for costs. Warner v. Turner , 18 B. М.
758 .

the court to be exercised in the further-
ance of justice, and when the party to
whom assignment is made is substituted,
securityshouldbe requiredforpast and
future costs. Dougherty v. Smith, 4

(3) Assignee may be substitutedas
plaintiffor actionmay proceedin name

Western Bank v . Coldeway , 94 S. W. 1,

Staplesv.Shiver,122 S. W. 826; Atkins

fer. After a claim in suit has been

311.

ment for the benefit of his creditors , it is
not necessary to give defendant notice of
the substitution of the assignee as
plaintiff. Jewellv.Porter, 11 R. 162, 11

confers on assignee the right to be sub-
stitutedas plaintiffor to prosecuteac-
tion in name of plaintiffwithout such
substitution.Cantrellv.Hewlett,2 Bush
311 .

§ 34 [49to52] [Marriedwomen- actionsby and against.] 1. [Wife
may sue alone.] In actionsbetween husband and wife;in actions
concerning her separateproperty;and , in actionsconcerning her
general property [and in actionsfor the personal sufferingof or

againsta wife she and her husband must joinor be joinedas
plaintiffsor defendants.
§ 34. (1) Constructionof section.The
common law rule is in forceexcept in
the cases mentioned in this section, and
wife can not sue alone in any case not
embraced by it. A next friendcan not
prosecutea suitin the name of the in-
fant wife against the wishes of her in-
fanthusband. Anderson v.Anderson,11
Bush 327,but seeKy .Stat.,sec.2128.
(2) The only objectof thissection
(49,same as sec.34-1of thisCode) isto
dispensewith the necessityfor a next
friend where the action concerns the
separatepropertyof thewife,or isbe-
tween herself and husband . It confers
no new right. Matson v.Matson ,4 Met .
262,and seeKy . Stat., sec.2128, confer-
ring on wife power to sue and be sued
alone in all cases .

debt or tort of wife made before mar-
riage, see Clark v. Miller, 88 Ky . 108,
10 S. W. 277, 10 R. 691; Medley v.

308; Fultz v . Fox , 9 B. M. 499 ; Beau-
mont v. Miller, 1 Met . 68; Agnew v.
Williams , 1 Bush 4 ; 6 Bush 34; 7 Bush
214; and see Ky . Stat., sec. 2130, fixing

fend,and shallhave the powers and rightswith referencethere-
to which he would have had but for such desertion .

shallnot be delayed by reason of the marriage.

fended by her committee or curator, ifshe have one, and ifshe

§ 35 [53,58] [Action- by whom brought.] 1.[Personunder disabil-
ity.] Exceptingmarried women . The actionof a personwho is
under disabilitymust be brought by his guardian, curator, or
committee, if he have one residing in this State, unless it be
againsthis guardian, curator, or committee ; or unless said guar-
dian, curator, or committee refuseto sue, in which case he must

herein, or his action against his curator, may be brought by
him , or, with his consent, by his next friend.

4. [Non -residentinfantor person of unsound mind .] The ac-

in a foreigncountry,and who has a guardian,curator,or com-
mitteeresidingtherein,may be broughtby such guardian,cur-
ator, or committee, or by his next friend.

3. [Defenseforbeforejudgment against- practice.] No judg-

covert- nor against a person of unsound mind , who is sum-

ants. It is the duty of the court to

with Code .

§ 38. [Guardianad litem- appointment duties- compensation.] 1.
[When may beappointed.] No appointmentofa guardianad litem
shallbe made untilthe defendant issummoned ,or untila person
is summoned for him , as is authorized by section52 , nor until
an affidavitof the plaintiff, or of his attorney, be filedin court,
or with the clerk, or presented to the judge during vacation,
showing that the defendant has no guardian, curator, nor com-
mittee, residingin thisState,known to the affiant.
2. [56,60,61] [Who may be and how appointed- removal of.]
A guardian ad litem must be a regular, practicingattorney
of the court;and may be appointedby thecourt,or thejudge
thereof, whether a guardian,curator, or committee appear for
the defendant or not ;or by the clerkof the court,during vaca-
tion, if the affidavitmentioned in subsection one of this section
be filed. The guardian ad litem may be appointed upon the
motion of the plaintiffor of any friend of the defendant ; but
neithertheplaintiffnor hisattorneyshallbe appointed,nor be
permitted to suggest the name of the proposed guardian ad
litem; and the court or judge may change the guardian so ap-
pointed whenever the interestof the infant may appear to re-
quire such change .

duty of theguardianad litemto attendproperlyto the prepa-
rationof the case;and in an ordinaryactionhe may causeas
many witnessesto be subpoenaed as he may think proper, sub-
jectto the controlof the court;and in an equitableaction he
may take depositions, not, however, exceedingthree, without
leave of the court .

§ 41-46] [Title IVManner of Commencing an Action .
§ 41 [67] [Summons toany county- costs.] A summons shallbe
issuedat any time,to any county,againstany defendant,at the
plaintiff'srequest. But a summons not served shallnot be taxed
in the costs unless otherwise ordered by the court.
§ 42 [68] [Copyforeachdefendant.] With everysummons theclerk
shallissue a copy thereoffor each defendant named therein, un-
lessotherwiseordered by the plaintiff.
§ 43 [69] [Time fixedinforreturn.] The time fixedin the sum-
mons for the defendant to answer shallbe the day the summons
is returnable. (When answer must be filed,sec.102.)
§ 44 [70] [Returnday ofsummons.] The summons shallbe return-
able to the firstday of the next term of the court, which does not
begin within ten days from the date of the summons . (See prac-
ticeact,sec.367а.)
§ 45 [71] [Deliverytoofficer.] The summons and copiesshallbe
delivered to the sheriff or other officer authorized to execute it.
(Sheriff to indorse time of reception, sec. 674 ; officersauthorized
to execute,secs.47, 667.)
§ 46 [72] [Sherifftoattendclerk'sofficedaily.] Itshallbe theduty
of the sheriffor of one of his deputies, to attend at the clerk's
officedaily- Sundays excepted to receiveany process that may
be issued,and the clerkshalldeliverto him any processremaining
in his office.

back of summons is valid . Northern

§ 44. (1) Construction of section and
holdingit to be directoryand not man-
datory; L. & N. R. R. Co. v. Smith, 87

in time. Ormsby v. Louisville, 79 Ky .

Plaintiffhas a rightto have summons
directedto and executedby any officer
authorizedto execute, at his option.

directedto sheriffor other officermay
be executed by constable or any officer
to whom it might have been directed.

secs. 47, 667, and notes thereto.

§ 47 [73] [Summons- who may serve.] [What officermay serve.]
The summons may be served- 1. By the officerto whom it is di-
rected, or any officerto whom it might have been directed, and
hisreturn thereonshallbe proof ofthe time and manner of service.
2. [Serviceby person appointedby officer.] By any person
appointedby the officerto whom itisdirected,by an indorse-
ment on the summons ;and the affidavitof such person indorsed
thereon shallbe proof of the time and manner of service.
(What officersmay serve,sec.667;courtmay appointperson,sec. 668; specialbailiffmay execute, Ky . Stat., sec. 4562;
amendment of return,sec.49.)
§ 47. (1) Recital as to service in
judgment is not of itselfsufficientevi-
dence of service. McKay v . Mayes , 16
R. 862,29 S. W. 327; Long v.Montgom-
ery, 6 Bush 394 ; but is entitled to con-
siderableweight. Lemming v. Mullins,

bailiffto executea summons , and writ-
ten authority after service will not
render the service valid. Thompson v .
Moore ,91 Ky .80, 12 R. 664, 15 S. W. 6;
and see sec . 667 and notes thereto .
(3) There must be a substantially
strictcompliance with the statute in re-
turnsmade by a specialbailiff; the pre-
sumptions in favor of the return of an
officerdo not exist; the affidavitof the
specialbailiffmust be signedby him or
it will not be sufficient. Simms v .
Simms , 88 Ky . 642, 11 R. 131, 11 S. W.
665 ; but return showing how and when
summons was executed is sufficient.Bar-
bour v.Newkirk, 83 Ky .529,7 R. 555.
(4) Specialbailiffcan not execute
summons out of the county in which he
residesand is appointed. Lillard v.
Brannin, 91 Ky . 511, 13 R. 74, 16 S. W.
349; nor can he execute an attachment .
Menderson v. Specker, 79 Ky . 509, 3 R.
421. See further as to special bailiffs,
sec. 4562 Ky . Stat., in substance the
same as this section .
(5) When a specialbailiffreturnsa
summons "not found," new authorityin
writing is necessaryto enable him to

§ 48 [74] [Manner of serving.] Unless otherwiseprovided, the
summons shallbe servedby delivering,or ifacceptancebe refused,

execute an alias summons . Lillard v .

349 .
(6) Deputy sheriffcan appoint special
bailiffto execute summons , but appoint-
ment shouldbe inname ofprincipalby

(7) Presumption in favor of an offi-
cer's return does not arise when return
is made by a special bailiff. Lloyd v .
McCauley,14B.M. 535; andsee83Ky.529 , 7 R. 555; 1 Met . 18.
§ 48. (1) False return. Where a sum-
mons has been returned as executed,
when infactitwasnot,andjudgmentrendered thereon, the return is conclu-
sive, if the plaintiffacted in good faith,
and can not be impeached in proceeding
to set asidejudgment. Thomas v. Ire-
land, 88 Ky . 581, 11 R. 103, 11 S. W.
653 ; Everett v.Ragan , 1 R. 421; but if
false return has been made by mistake
of officerand defaultjudgment rendered
thereon, the return may be questioned
in proceedingto enjoin collectionof
judgment . Bramlett v. McVey , 91 Ky .
151, 12 R. 760, 15 S. W. 49; and see
Board v.Helm ,2 Met .500 .
(2) Fraud- serviceprocured by void.
Service of processupon defendant who
has been brought within the reach of
the court by the fraudulentrepresenta-
tionsof the plaintiffconfersno juris-diction to render judgment. Wood v.
Wood ,78 Ky . 624, 1 R. 358.
(3) Holiday - Sunday . It was held
under Code of 1854 that process executed
on Sunday was a nullity; that the holi-
day mentioned in that Code did not
mean Sunday . Moore v . Hagan , 2 Duv .
437. This Code (sec.665) uses the word
" Sunday" as well as holiday.
(4 ) "Not found ." Return of not to be
made until officer has visited residence
of defendant . Ky . Stat., sec. 4566 .
(5) Officerswho can execute. Sum-
mons directed to a sheriff or constable
may be executedby any officerto whom
it might have been directed. Long v.
Gaines,4 Bush 353; Boaz v.Nail,2 Met .
245; Russellv.Durham , 16 R. 516,29 S.
W. 16 ; and secs. 47, 667 .
(6 ) Return of officer- presumption-
quashing - practice. Summons returned
"executed" with no signatureto the re-
turn is not sufficient evidence of service
without otherproof. Long v.Montgom-
ery, 6 Bush 394.
(7 ) Return of "executed on J. H. C.
by readingthewithin," isnotsufficient
return on summons . When the officer
returnssummons executed,the presump-
tionwillbe,itisdone accordingto law;
but when the return shows how it was
executed,thatpresumptioncannotarise.
Case v . Colston, 1 Met . 145; Webber v .
Webber , 1 Met . 18 ; 1 Duv . 251 .
(8) Summons returned "executed by
deliveringa truecopytoJohnLong,"will be presumed to have been executed
on the defendant John Long. Long v.
Gaines , 4 Bush 353. And if summons is
lost, thiscourt willratherpresume that
itwas executedthan thatjudgment wasrendered without service of process.
Jones v. Edwards , 78 Ky . 6; 6 R. 523;
Berry v.Foster,22 R. 745.
(9) The presumption in favor of a
returnmade by an officerdoes not arise
when the returnismade by one not an
officera specialbailiff. Lloyd v. Mc-
Cauley,14 B. M. 535. See alsoBarbour
v. Newkirk , 83 Ky . 529, 7 R. 555, and
notes to sec. 47 .
(10) Mistake in date of execution-
presumption. King v. Spearman, 3 B.
Μ . 289 .
(11) Officer'sreturn is not conclusive
before judgment, and defendant may
contradict it and show such defect as
willprevent court from taking jurisdic-
tion. Motions or answer filedto show
want of jurisdictionwillnot enter ap-
pearance. Barbour v. Newkirk , 83 Ky .
529 ,7 R. 555 ; C. & O. R. R. Co. v .Heath ,
87 Ky . 651, 10 R. 646, 9 S. W. 832; and
cases cited in note 9 , sec. 39 .
(12) Where the petitionstated who
the mother of infant defendants was ,
and that she was their custodian, the
failure of officerto state in return that
he executed summons on mother and
custodiandoes not invalidatejudgment.
Bailey v. Fanning, 12 R. 644, 14 S. W.
908 ; and see notes to sec. 52 .
(13) Reading summons to defendant
is not sufficient service . The officer must

(4 ) Guardian ad litem- service on .

Ky . 650.

ient. Palmer v . Husbands , 119 S. W.

though under twenty-one,may be served

(9 ) Return of officer. In action
againstan infantwhose fatherwas dead,

mons was executed upon infant and his
mother, is sufficient, nothing appearing

It willbe presumed officerdid hisduty.
Webber v. Webber , 1 Met . 18; Rodgers
v.Rodgers, 17 R. 358,31 S. W. 139; and

9 R. 761, 6 S. W. 595; Baileyv.Fanning,

535 .

mons against infant defendants , that he

595 .

mons on mother and custodian does not

ning, 12 R. 644, 14 S. W. 908; Rodgers
v. Rodgers , 17 R. 358, 31 S. W. 139.
(13) Amendment of return, allowable
even after judgment . Tyler v . Jewell,

§ 59 ] [Title IVService of Summons - Constructive Service .
§ 59 [440,441] [Attorney- appointment, duties, compensation.] 1.
[Clerktoappointattorney.] The clerk,atthetimeofmaking such
warning order, shallappoint, as attorneyfor the defendant, a
regular, practicingattorneyof the court; but the court may , and
for any reason which itdeems good shall, appoint another attor-
ney as a substituteforthe attorneyappointed by theclerk. Neither
the plaintiffnor his attorneyshallbe appointed, or be permitted
to suggestthe name of,the defendant'sattorney.
(9) Warning order is sufficientto
bring a non-residentbefore the court
whether he be sane or insane . Harlam-
mert v.Moody , 15 R. 839, 26 S. W. 2 ;
and so when he has been absent from
State seven years, and it is not known
whether he be livingor dead. Hogue v.
Yeager,107 Ky .582,21 R. 1299,54 S.W.
961 .
(10) As to sufficiencyof warning or-
der made under United States statute,
see Mer . Trust Co. v. South Park Co.,
94 Ky .271, 15 R. 70,22 S. W. 314.
(11) The taking of warning order
against non -resident defendant is com-
mencement of action as to him ; no sum-
mons is necessary. Hoffman v . Brungs ,
83 Ky . 400; and if defendant is known
to be a non -resident the issual of a sum-
mons will not begin action- a warningorder is necessary. Traders Bank v .
Hoffman , 99 Ky . 240, 18 R. 148, 35 S.
W. 631 .
(12) Warning order made within a
reasonable time after affidavitfiled is
sufficient in this case warning order
was made day after affidavitwas filed.
Cornwall v.FallsCity Bank ,92 Ky .381,
13 R. 606, 18 S. W. 452 ; and see Ross
v. McGrath , 27 R. 723, 86 S. W. 555.
(13) Warning order should be signed
by the clerk and name the attorney ap-
pointed to defend. Jones v. Griffin, 25
R. 117 .
§ 59. (1) Appearance - appeal enters.
Prosecution of appeal by non-resident
enters appearance. Gill v. Johnson, 1
Met .649; Salterv.Dunn , 1 Bush 311; 4
Met . 341 .
(2) Person constructivelysummoned
who appearsforfirsttimeby appealing
from judgment againsthim willonly be
heldtohave appearedinlowercourton
day mandate is entered, and no judg-
ment can be rendered against him at
that term. Beazely v. Maret , 1 Bush
466 .
(3) An attorney for the plaintiff
should not in any case be allowed to
enter the appearance of the defendant.
Ball v. Poor, 81 Ky . 26, 4 R. 746; and
see notes to sec . 410 .
(4 ) Answer enters appearance . Non-
resident who by his attorney filesan an-
swer sworn to by him in another State
must be regarded as personallybefore
the court, although he disclaims in an-
swer any intentionof enteringhis ap-
pearance. Tipton v. Wright, 7 Bush .
448 .
(5) Attorney employed by non-resi-
dent should so statein his report, and
when he fails to do this it will not be
presumed that he has been employed.
Christiev.Garrity, 14 R. 910, 22 S. W.
158 .
(6 ) No allowance should be made to
attorney for non-residentappointedby
the court when he is acting under con-
tract with the non -resident and looking
to him forcompensation. Cabellv.Ca-
bell, 1 Met . 319.
(7) No allowance should be made to
an attorney appointed to defend for non-
residentwhen no reportis filedby him
or duty performed . Southwood v . My-
ers, 3 Bush 681 ; Jackson v . McElroy , 2
Bush 132 .
(8 ) Attorney must state in his report
that he has carefully examined the case
and is unable to make defense unless
as authorized by sub -sec. 7, he can
make defense. Dineen v. Hall, 112 Ky .
273, 23 R. 1615, 65 S. W. 445 ; Ramsey
v . Kieth , 25 R. 582, 76 S. W. 142.
(9 ) Failure to appoint- effectof. The
court say : "And , although the failure
to appoint the attorney (for non-resi-
dent ) or to take the bond required by
sec. 440 (410) are reversibleerrors, the
jurisdictionbeing complete, the judg-
ment will not be void ." Thomas v . Ma-
hone , 9 Bush 111.
(10) Guardian ad litem- In Ramsey v.
Keith,25 R. 582,76 S. W. 142,the court
holds that it is proper to appoint a
guardian ad litem for a non -resident in-
fant,but failureto do so willnot render
judgment void. Myers v. Pedigo,24 R.
1923 , 72 S. W. 734 .

Title IV ] [§ 59Service of Summons - Constructive Service.
2. [Duty of attorney and report by.] Such attorney must
make diligenteffortsto inform the defendant,by mail, concern-
ing the pendency and nature of the action against him , and
must report to the court,during the firstterm, which does not
commence withinsixtydays afterhisappointment, the result
of his efforts .
3. [Powers of attorney limited.] The duties and powers of
such attorneyare governed by the lastforegoingparagraph
unlesshe be employed by the defendant;or unlesshe be unable
to inform the defendant concerning the action; or unless he
learn that the defendant isunder disability, other than covert-
ure, or other than infancy and coverturecombined .
4. [Employment ofattorney- effectof.] Such attorney,ifem-
ployed by the defendant, shallso report to the court;and such
report shall be treated as an appearance of the defendant in
the action .
5. [Affirmative defense- when attorney to make .] If such
attorneycan not inform the defendantconcerningthe action,
or ifhe learn that the defendant isunder disability, other than
coverture, or other than infancy and coverture combined , he
shallso report to the court, and shallmake an affirmativede-
fenseifhe can ; or ifhe can not make such defense,he shallso
reportto the court,and shallbe subject, with referenceto such
report, to the provisionsof subsection3 of section36 ; and no
act of his shallbe treatedas an appearance of such defendant.
6. [Compensation of attorney.] The provisionsof subsection
4 of section38 apply with referenceto the compensation of such
attorneys. (See furtheras tofee,Ky .Stat.,sec.1742.)
7. [Judgment when nottobe rendereduntildefenseorreport
made .] No judgment shallbe rendered against such defendant
ifunder any disability, otherthan coverture, or infancyand
coverture combined , until a defense or report shall have been
filedpursuanttosubsection5 ofthissection. (Executingbond
for attached property is appearance, sec. 690 ; personal judg-
ment can not be rendered,sec.419.)
(11) Judgment - attorney appointed
for non -residenthas no authority to con-
senttojudgment againsthim unlessem •
ployed by him ; and recitalin judgment
that it is renderedby consentwillnot
sustain it. Anderson v . Sutton , 2 Duv .
480; and see Ball v. Poor, 81 Ky . 26, 4
R. 746 .
(12) Judgment not to be rendered
untildefenseor reportis filed. Powell
v.Baer, 143 Ky .282, 136 S. W. 629.
(13) Report of attorney. Failureof
attorney appointed to defend for non-
residenttomake defenseor filereportis
not reversibleerror, and does not affect
validityof steps taken by the court.
Brown v. Early, 2 Duv . 369; Ball v.
Poor, 81 Ky . 26, 4 R. 746; Morrison v.
Beckham ,96 Ky .72, 16 R. 294,27 S. W.
868; but seeDineen v.Hall, 112Ky .273,
23 R. 1615, 65 S. W. 445; holdingit is
errorto order saleof property without
proper report from attorney. And to
same effectis Ramsey v. Kieth, 25 R.
582, 76 S. W. 142.

§ 60-61] [Title IVService of Summons - Constructive Service .
§ 60 [91] [Defendantdeemedsummoned,when- effect.] A defend-
ant against whom a warning order is made and for whom an
attorney has been appointed shall be deemed to have been con-
structivelysummoned on the thirtiethday thereafter, and the
actionmay proceed accordingly. (See practiceact, sec. 367a;
as torulein courtsofcontinuoussession,seesec.1004,page 638.)
§ 61 [92] [Actualserviceondefendant- effect.] The plaintiffmay ,at any time beforejudgment,have a summons servedon the de-
fendantif found in thisState, althougha warning order may
have been previouslyentered against him ; and aftersuch service

§ 96 [126,128] [Counter-claim, set-off, cross-petition.] 1. [Counter-
claim defined.] A counter-claim isa cause of action in favor of a
defendantagainsta plaintiff,or againsthim and another,which
arisesout of the contract,or transactions,statedin the petition
Os the foundationof theplaintiff'sclaim,or which isconnected
with the subjectof the action. (See further, secs.99,372. See

2358a .)
2. [Set-offdefined.] A set-offisa cause of actionarisingupon
a contract,judgment or award in favor of a defendant against
a plaintiff, or against him and another; and it can not be
pleaded exceptin an actionupon a contract,judgment or award .
(See further,secs.97,372, 377. See as to noticewhen lienon
realproperty is asserted,Ky .Stat.,sec.2358а.)
3. [Cross-petitiondefined.] A cross-petitionisthe commence-
ment of an action by a defendant against a co-defendant, or a
person who isnot a party to the action,or against both ; or by
a plaintiffagainsta co-plaintiff,or a personwho isnot a party
to the action,or againstboth ;and isnot allowed to a defendant,
except upon a cause of action which affects, or is affectedby ,
the originalcause of action; nor to a plaintiff,exceptupon a
cause of actionwhich affects,or isaffectedby,a set-offor coun-
ter-claim. (In what pleadingscounter-claim and set-offal-
lowed,sec.111 ;trialof,sec.372 ;judgment on,sec.387 ;in what
pleading cross-petitionallowed,secs.111 ; 692. See as to notice
when lienon realpropertyisasserted,Ky .Stat.,sec.2358a.)

gan v.Booth, 13 Bush 480; nor is a de-
nialin the conjunctivegood. 81 Ky .458,

nial that defendant is indebted as al-

COUNTER -CLAIM .
§ 96. (1) Counter-claim. "First,those
causes of actionwhich ariseout of the
contractset forthin the plaintiff'speti-
tion as the foundation of his claim;
secondly, thosecausesof actionarising
95
out of the transaction set forth in the
petitionas the foundationof the plain-
tiff'sclaim; and, thirdly,thosecausesof

page 602. Counter -claim is an action
(sec. 732-34) and may be presentedin

tain the words "answer and counter-
claim" (sec. 97-4). Nutter v . Johnson ,

that itshallbe a cause of actionarising

plaintiff'sdemand ; or that it be con-
nected with the subjectof the action.
It is not required that the counter -claim

ariseout of the contractset forth in
the petition, but it is sufficientthat it
arisesout of the transactionsset forth
in the petition or be connected with the
subjectof the action. Allenv. Hodge,

SET -OFF .
(1) Administrator - distributee. Ad-
ministratoror heirsmay plead as set-off
against distributee'sinteresthis indebt-

(2) Assignee- against. A set-offto be
available against an assignee must have
existed before notice of the assignment .
A debt or demand can not be used as a
set-off before it becomes due , and unless
itisdue beforenoticeof the assignment,
it is not availableas a set-off against
the assignee. A surety,untilhe pays the
money for his principal, has no demand
against him which can be pleaded as a
set-off,even ifthe principalisinsolvent.
Walker v. McKay , 2 Met . 294; 1 Met .
112; and see Chenault v. Bush , 84 Ky .

Bk .v. Lagow ,3 R. 173; nor willa party
who has notice that his note has been
assigned, although he may not know to
whom , be allowed to buy up claims
against assignor and set them offagainst
note. Small v. Browder, 11 B. M. 213;
but the defendant may after suit is filed
buy validclaimsagainstthe plaintiffand
plead them as a set-off. Overby v.Wells,
21 R. 1316,54 S. W. 955.
(3) Assignment of note to be used as.
Defendant can plead as a set-offa note
of the plaintiffassignedto him under a
contractto use the note as a set-off,and
account to the assignor only for the
amount he can make in that way , and
reassignthe balance remaining unpaid.
Graham v.Tilford,1 Met .112; Dorsey v.
Reese, 14 B. M. 157; McBrayer v.Dean ,
100 Ky . 398, 18 R. 847, 38 S. W. 508;
and see Overby v. Wells, 21 R. 1316,
54 S. W. 955. But where a note is
so assigned, to be used as a set-off, a
court of equity, where the rights of
others than the party who owes the note
intervene, would be reluctant to sanction
sucha speculationby aidingthe partymaking theventuretodefeatan acknowl-
edged equity. Such contractsare not to
befavored,butwherethedebtoraloneisaffected, he willnot be heard to com-
plain. Tinley v. Martin, 80 Ky . 463, 4
R. 327 .
(4) Commonwealth - actionby. In a

make such defense as will defeat re-
covery, but can not get judgment over.

174 .

ment against the sheriffobtained a lien

§ 97 ] [Title VIICounter-Claim, Set-Off,Cross-Petition.
§ 97. [Proceedingsupon counter-claims, cross-petitions, set-offs-
caption.] 1.[Applicationof,sec.90.] The provisionsofsection90
concerningthe demand and givingof reliefapply to set-offs,
counter-claims,and cross-petitions.
2. [Summons not requiredupon set-offor counter-claim.] No
summons is required upon a set-offor counter-claim against a
plaintiff.
3. [127,129] [Proceedingsupon againstnew parties.] Proceed-
ings upon cross petitions,and upon set-offsand counter-claims
againstnew parties,shallbe the same as those upon petitions;
but they shallnot delay the trialof any issue in the original
action, or upon a set-offor counter-claim, concerning which a
judgment can be rendered without prejudiceto the rightsof the
defendantstothecross-petition,or to the rightsof new parties
to a set-off or counter -claim .
4. [Caption to contain nature of action.] A defendant shall
not have judgment upon a set-offor counter-claim, unless the
captionof the answer containthe words, answer and set-off,
or the words,answer and counter-claim; but a misdescription
in thecaption,ofthenatureofthedefendant'sclaims,shallnot
preventhim from having judgment;nor shalla plaintiffhave
judgment upon a counter-claimunlessthecaptionof hisreply
contain the words , reply and counter-claim.
§ 97. Statementin caption- waiver.
By replyingtoanswerandcounter-claim,
and joiningissuethereon, the plaintiff
waived therightto objectto theplead-
ing,becauseitwas not designatedin the
captionas an answer and counter-claim;
iftherehad beenno reply,andjudgment
had gone by default, judgment would
have been erroneous. Cason v . Cason ,
79 Ky .558,3 R. 367; Nutterv.Johnson,
80 Ky .426, 4 R. 305; Lacey v. Lacey,
95 Ky . 110, 15 R. 439, 23 S. W. 673;
Kellyv.Talbott,19R.632,41 S.W. 439;
Salyerv.Napier,21 R. 172,51 S. W. 10;
Arthurs v. Thompson , 97 Ky . 218, 17 R.
118,30 S.W. 628; Gordonv.CityNat.
Bank ,140Ky .47,130 S. W. 818.

§ 98 [132,133] [Replymay contain- what.] A replymay contain-
1. [Traverse.] A traverse.

factswhich constitutean estoppelagainst,or avoidanceof, a
set-off, counter-claim or defense stated in the answer .
3. [Counter-claim.] A counter-claim against a set-off.
4. [Cross-petition.] A cross-petition. (Forms of,pages694-96,
when not allowed, sec.112.)

averment in the reply. Mitchellv.Ash-

trial; and to an equitableaction on the third day of term, ifthe
summons be served in the county in which the action is brought
ten days,or elsewherein the Statetwenty days,or out of the State
sixtydays,beforethe commencement of the term,or ifthe defend-
ant be constructivelysummoned thirtydays beforethecommence-
ment of the term. (See practiceact, sec. 367a; when defendant
constructivelysummoned may filedefense, sec.408 ; time of trial
in ordinary actions,secs.359,363.)
§ 103 [136] [Ordinaryaction- timetoplead.] In an ordinaryac-
tion, allother pleadingsmust be filedon or before the callingof
the case for trial.
§ 104 [138] [Equitableaction- timetoanswer.] Ifthesummons in
an equitable action be served before the commencement of the
term, but not the length of time mentioned in section102, the
defenseshallbe filedin thirtydays afterthe service.
§ 105 [139] [Equitableaction- timetocompletepleadings.] In an
equitableaction,ifthe answer be due and be filedon the thirdday
of the term, the reply shallbe filedwithin three days thereafter,
and the subsequent pleadingsshallbe filedwithin threedays after
the filingof thereply. (Time oftrial,secs.360,364 to 367. See
PracticeAct ,sec.367a.)
§ 106.[Equitableaction- timetocompletepleading.] In an equi-
tableaction,ifthe answer be not due and filedon the thirdday of
the term, the replyshallbe filedwithinten days afternoticeof
the filingof the answer , and the subsequent pleadings shall be
filedwithin ten days afternoticeof the filingof the reply.

§ 107-109] [Title VIITime of Pleading .
§ 107 [140] [Time to pleadextended.] The courtmay , for good
cause shown ,and at the costof the party applying therefor,extend
thetime forfilinga pleading,but not beyond thenext succeeding
term .
§ 108 [141] [Filingin vacation- notice- effect.] The filingof an
answer or subsequent pleading in the clerk's officeduring vaca-
tion, within the time allowed, with noticethereofto the adverse
party,or his attorney,shallbe equivalentto a filingin court.
§ 109. [Filingdemurrerin vacation- curingdefect- waiver.] 1.[De-
murrer in vacation.] A party who filesan answer or subsequent
pleading during vacationmay filea demurrer therewith.
2. [Amendment to cure defect.] The adverseparty may ,dur-
ing that vacation, and afternotice to the party filingthe de-
murrer , or his attorney, filean amended pleading to cure any
defectsuggested by the demurrer .
3. [Waiver ofdemurrer.] The failureofa party,who so files
a specialdemurrer , to presentitfor consideration, at the next
succeedingterm of the court,shallbe a waiver of any defect
stated in the demurrer , except that to the jurisdictionof the
court of the subjectof the action.
§ 107. Time to fileanswer . On mo-
tion for continuance and time to filean-
swer the affidavitshould disclosethe na-
ture of the defense so that the court may
judge whether or not it is sufficient.
Engleman v.NationalBank ,2 Bush 165.

§ 110 [118,125] [Captionof pleading- what itshallstate- petitions
how described.] 1. [Caption,what to be statedin.] The caption of
a pleading must statethe name of the court in which the action
isbroughtor pending,and the names of theparties,designating
who are plaintiffsand who are defendants,coupledwith a word,
or short phrase, describingthe character of the pleading, with
thisexception: If therebe severalpartieson eitherside, a state-
ment of the name of the plaintiff,or defendant,firstnamed in the
petition, followedby the phrase, "etc.," shallsuffice, except in
petitions,cross-petitionsand answerswhich make new partiesto
a set-off or counter -claim .
2. [Petitions,how described.] Petitionsin ordinaryactions
shallbe describedby the word ,"petition" ;thosein other actions
shallbe describedby thewords,"petitionin equity." (See sec.
97.)

§ 111-113] [Title VIIGeneral Rules of Pleading.
§ 111.[Pleadingthatmay bemade cross-petition- set-off- counter-
claim.] 1. [Cross-petition- pleadingthatmay be.] No pleading,
exceptan answer toan originalpetition,or theplaintiff'sreplyto
such answer ,shallbe made a cross-petition.
2. [Set-off- pleading that may be.] No pleading, except an
answer to an originalpetition,shallpresenta set-off.
3. [Counter-claim- pleadingthatmay be.] No pleading,ex-
ceptan answer to an originalpetition, or a replyto a set-off,
shallpresenta counter-claim. (These pleadingsdefined, sec.
96 ;proceedingsupon ,sec.97.)
§ 112.[Replyand additionalpleading- when notallowed.] There
shallbe no reply, nor additionalpleading, exceptto affirmative
allegationsof an adversepleading. (What thesepleadingsmay
contain,sec.98 to 101.)
§ 113 [124] [Generalprovisionsconcerningpleadings.] 1.[Demurrer
topartofpleading.] A partymay demur topartofa pleading,and
present an issueof fact as to another part.
2. [119] [What pleadingmay contain.] A pleadingmay con-
tain statements of as many causes of action,legalor equitable,
and of as many mattersof estoppeland of avoidance, legalor
equitable, totalor partial; and may make as many traverses;
and may present as many demurrers as theremay be grounds
for in behalf of the pleader.
3. [119,125] [Paragraphingpleading- duty ofcourt.] If there
be more than one,each must be distinctlystatedin a separate,
numbered paragraph;and either,which isintendedto respond
to partonlyof an adversepleading,must show towhat partit
isresponsive. It istheduty of thecourt,upon or withoutmo-
tion,to enforcetheseprovisions;and,forthatpurpose,to dis-
miss an actionwithoutprejudice,or tostrikea pleadingor any
partthereof,from thecase,or toallowa new pleading.
(4) Defendant sued by wrong name
must disclose his correct name if he
makes objectionon account of the mis-
nomer . L. & N. R. R. v . Hall,12 Bush

can only be assertedin the pleadings
mentioned in this section . Fritts v .

§ 114.[Materialissuetobe formed- dutyofcourt.] Partiesmust,
beforetrial, form a material issueconcerning each cause of con-
troversy;and itistheduty of thecourt,upon or withoutmotion,
to compel them to do so; and , for that purpose, they may be re-
quired to reform theirpleadings,or the pleading of a party who is
in faultmay be strickenfrom the case.
§ 115 [142] [Written in Englishlanguage and signed- exception.]
Pleadings must be in the English language, and as concise as
possibleconsistentlywith clearness; and , excepting those men-
tionedin section705,must be in writing, and be signedby the
partieswho filethem , or by theirattorneys.

affidavit to the effect that the affiant believes that the statements
ofthepleadingaretrue,excepting(See furtheras to verification,
secs. 137-139. Forms of verification, page 699.)

ithad not been filed.

§ 121 [147] [Irrelevantorredundantmatterstrickenout.] Irrelevant
or redundant matter in a pleading shallbe strickenout,upon or

fora fullstatementofpracticeregarding

§ 129 [156] [Variancewhen material- amendment .] No variancebe-
tween pleadingsand proofismaterial,which does not misleada
party,tohisprejudice,inmaintaininghisactionor defenseuponthemerits. A partywho claimstohave beenso misledmust show
thatfactto the satisfactionof the court;and ,thereupon,the court
may orderthe pleadingto be amended,upon such terms as may
be just. (See further,secs.130,131.)
§ 129. (1) Material variance. Where
petition alleged that certain property
was purchasedfromdefendantand proofshowed that itwas a purchaseof double
the amount from defendantand another
the varianceis fatal. Gray v.Garrison,
2 R. 218; and see further, Bannisterv.
Weatherford, 7 B. M. 271; Howard v.
Chiles, 8 B. Μ .377.
(2) Judgment can not be renderedagainst a defendant as an individual
when thepetitionshowsthathe executedthe obligationsued on in his fiducial

derson , 1 Met . 426 .
(3) Plaintiffinstitutedsuit upon a
note executed by the defendant to an-
otherperson,and allegedthatthe note
had been executed to himself; it had not
beenassignedto plaintiff. Held thatthe
variancewas fatal, and plaintiffcould
not recover. Dodd v.King,1 Met .430.
(4) In an actionupon an expresscon-
tractno recoverycan be had on proof of
an impliedcontractin the absenceof an
amendment conformingthe pleadingsto
theproof. Newton v. Field,98 Ky . 186,
17 R. 769,32 S. W. 623; and see further,
Bull v.McCrea ,8 B. M. 422.
(5) Negligence. The allegationofwill-ful negligencenecessarilyincludesall
inferiorgrades, and, upon failingto
establishhisrightto punitivedamages,plaintiffmay recovercompensatorydam-
ages, ifthe defendantisa railroadcom-
pany and the person injuredwas not at
the time an employe. L., C. & L. R. R.
Co.v.Case,9 Bush 728; Claxtonv.L. &
B. S. R. R. Co., 13 Bush 636. And a gen-
eral allegationthat injury was caused
by negligenceand carelessnessof de-
fendant is sufficient. Chiles v . Drake , 2
Met . 146 .
(6) Negligence- where plaintiffspeci-
fiesthecharacterof negligencehe relies
on he can not recoverby showing a dif-
ferentstateof negligence. McCain v. L.
& N. R. R. Co.,13 R. 809,18 S.W. 537;Thomas v. L. & N. R. R., 18 R. 164, 35
S. W. 910 ; nor can he prove a different
state of negligence from that set out in
petition. Greer v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 94
Ky .169,14 R. 876,21 S.W. 649.(7) Practice. The propermode to take
advantageof a variancebetweenallega-tionandproofisa motiontoinstructthe
jury as in case of a non-suit. Mitcher-
son v.Grays, 4 B. M. 400.

§ 140 [167] [Equitableactionsin- answersreadas deposition.] In
equitableactions,a party may annex to his pleadingwritten inter-
rogatoriesto the adverseparty,concerning any materialallegation
thereof;and answers thereto,on oath,may be read by eitherparty,
as a depositionbetween the party interrogatingand the party
answering .
§ 141 [168] [Partyansweringnotconfinedtomere response.] The
party answering shallnot be confinedto responding merely to the
interrogatories, but may stateany factsconcerning the cause of
action to which the interrogatoriesrefer, and they may likewise
be read as a deposition. (If answers statenew matter; how con-
troverted, sec. 608.)
§ 142 [169] [Time to answer- effectof delayon trial.] Interrog-
atoriesannexed to a petitionshallbe answeredwhen thepartyis
requiredto answer the petition;ifannexed to any other pleading,
they shallbe answered in twenty days afternoticeof the filing
thereofshallbe given to the adverse party or his attorney; but if
answered twenty days beforethe term at which the actionstands
regularlyfortrial, theactionshallnot be postponedon account
of theirnot beingsooner answered. (When party interrogated
can not demand trial,sec.365.)
§ 140. Interrogatories.Party answer-
ing interrogatoriesmay , on the trialof
the action,read his answers as a deposi-
tion. Ecklar v. Galbreath, 5 Bush 617;
Short v. Tinsley, 1 Met . 397. But they
can not be so read unlessauthenticated,
as requiredby theCode.Fordv.Thomp-
son, i Met. 580; seeMusick v.Ray, 3
Met . 428. Defendant is not bound to
answer interrogatorycontainedin body
of petition nor unless annexed to it.
Burnett v.Garnett,18 B. M. 68; McCrae
v.Gunther,14 R. 5,18 S.W. 1034; nor to
answer any question which would sub-
jecthim to criminalor penalprosecu-
tion. Colev.Wilson, 18B.М. 212.

Title VII ] [§ 143-49Interrogatories.
§ 143 [170] [Ordinaryactions- when allowedin.] In ordinaryac-
tions,a partymay annex to hispleadingwritteninterrogatories
to an adverse party concerning any material allegation-
1. [If partyresidesmore than twenty milesfrom court.] If
the party interrogateddo not residewithin twenty miles from
the place where the actionmay be pending.
2. [Ifparty unable to attendcourt.] If the party interrogated
be unable to attend court on account of infirmityor imprison-
ment , or be a female .
§ 144 [171] [Ordinaryactions- time to answer.] In ordinaryac-
tions, interrogatoriesannexed to the petitionshall be answered
when the petitionis required to be answered ; if annexed to any
other pleading, they shallbe answered at or beforethe callingof
the cause for trial,ifthe party interrogatedhad reasonablenotice
of their being filed.
§ 145 [172] [Trialnotpostponedbecauseoffailuretoanswer.] The
trialof an ordinary actionshallnot be postponed on account of
the failureto answer the interrogatories,ifthe party interrogated
be presentin court at the trial,so thathe may be orallyexamined ;
nor, in case of his absence, without an affidavitshowing the facts
which the party believeswill be proved by the answers thereto,
and thatthe party has not filedthe interrogatories,nor omitted to
filethem , for the purpose of delay; nor ifthe party will consent
that the facts stated in the affidavit shall be considered as admitted
by him .
§ 146 [173] [Applicationofsection141.] The provisionsofsection
. 141 shall apply to the answers to interrogatoriesin ordinary
actions. (If answers state new matter; how controverted,
sec. 609.)
§ 147 [174] [Answers to distinguishbetween knowledgeand in-
formation.] A party,in answeringsuch interrogatories,shalldis-
tinguishclearlybetween what isstatedfrom his personal knowl-
edge and what isstatedfrom informationor beliefmerely. An
unqualifiedstatement of a factshallbe consideredas made from
hispersonalknowledge .
§ 148 [175] [Verificationofanswers- what tobe statedin.] Answers
to such interrogatoriesshallbe verifiedby the affidavitof the
party answering,to the effectthat the statementsin them made of
his personal knowledge are true, and those made from the in-
formation of others he believes to be true .
§ 149 [176] [Party- when compelledto appearas witness.] If a
party residewithintwenty milesfrom theplacein which the ac-
tionmay be pending,he may be summoned by the adverseparty,
and be compelledto testifyon thetrialof an ordinaryactionas
any other witness.

§ 150 [177] [Claimordefensewhen sustainedby failuretoanswer.]
If a party, filinginterrogatories, filean affidavitthat he verily
believesthat any subjectof the interrogatoriesisin the personal
knowledge of the adverse party, and that his answers thereto, if
trulymade ,willsustainthe affiant'sclaim or defense,or any part
thereof, and the adverse party shallfail,afterreasonabletime, to

in such affidavit, shallbe deemed to be sustained.
§ 151 [178] [Courtmay compelanswers- penaltyforfailure.] The
court may compel answers to interrogatoriesby process of con-
tempt,and may ,on the failureof the party to answer them , after
reasonabletime dismiss the petition, or strikeout the pleading,
of a party so failing. (In action on constructiveservicecourt
may propound ,sec. 412.)

MOTION TO VACATE AN ORDER OF ARREST .
Application for reduction of bail or vacation of order.

§ 177 [204] [Applicationforreductionof bailor vacationof order.]
A defendant,againstwhom an order of arresthas been obtained,
may , at any time before judgment in the action, and before a
decisionof a motion on account of the insufficiencyof the bail,
apply to the court, or, in vacation and before executing a bail
bond , to the judge thereof, or to any circuitjudge, or to the
presidingjudge of the county court,to vacate the order of arrest,
or to reduce the amount of bail. Reasonable notice of the appli-
cation shallbe given to the plaintiff. If satisfiedthat the bail
ought not to have been required,or that the sum for which itwas
demanded is too large, the court, or judge, may vacate the order
of arrest, or reduce the amount of bail. The decisionof the mo-
tion shallbe finalin the action, but shallnot affectthe rightsof
the parties in any other action. Upon the vacation of the order
of arrest, the defendant shallbe discharged, or the bail bond , if
any be given,shallbe canceled. (Notice may be served on attor-
ney,sec.631.)
§ 178 [205] [Motionsupportedoropposedby affidavits.] If themo-
tionbe supported by affidavitsor other evidenceout of the record,
the plaintiffmay oppose itby affidavitsor other evidence,in addi-
tion to that on which the order of arrest was obtained . (Person
on whose affidavitorder issuedmay be examined ,sec. 548.)
§ 179 [206] [Scirefaciasagainstbailabolished.] The writ of scire
faciasagainstbailis abolished.
§ 177. Appeal does not lie from an
order vacating an order of arrestand
discharging the defendant . Porter v .
Griffin, 143 Ky . 138, 136 S. W. 130.

cient . Franklin Bank v . Bank of Wheel-
ing, 1 Met .156; and seeFuqua v.Farm-

provisionsofsection51,and by summoning thepersonor cor-
porationto answer as a garnisheein the action. The sheriff
shalldelivercopiesto,and summon ,such personsas garnishees
as theplaintiffmay direct. (What officer'sreturnmust show,
sec. 217.)

§ 204. [Duty of officeron whom executed- penalty.] It shall be
the duty of every officerupon whom an order of attachment may
be executedpursuantto section203,subsection2,to furnishan
inventoryand appraisement of the attachedproperty held by him ,
or copiesthereof,and a statement of the distressor processunder
which ,and of the sum for which itisheld,to the officerexecuting
the attachment ;and to hold so much of the attached property,or
of itsproceeds,as may not be necessaryto satisfysuch process
or distress,subjectto the order of the courtfrom which the attach-
ment issued;and his failureto perform eitherof thosedutiesmay
be punished by the court as a contempt .
§ 205 [229] [Garnishee- dutyof- penalty.] Itshallbe theduty of
every person mentioned in subsection3 of section203, to whom
the sheriffshallapply therefor,to furnishhim a certificateof the
number of sharesof the defendanť in the stock of the corporation,
or a descriptionof the property held by such corporationor per-
son, for the benefitof the defendant, or belonging to him , or the
amount of the debt owing to the defendant by such corporation
or person,whether due or not ;and a failureto perform thisduty
may be punishedby thecourtas a contempt.
§ 206 [230] [Personaltyto be firsttaken.] The defendant'sper-
sonal property shall be firsttaken under an attachment ; if
enough thereofbe not found,then his realproperty.
§ 207 [231] [Fund incourt- attachmentof.] Ifthepropertyto be
attached be a fund in court, the attachment shallbe executed by
leaving with the clerk of the court a copy thereof, with a no-
ticespecifyingthe fund ; and if severalorders of attachment be
executed upon such fund on the same day , they shall be satisfied
out of it ratably.
§ 208 [232] [Jointproperty- executionof bond by plaintiff.] The
sheriffshallnot,in executingan attachmentupon personalprop-
ertyheld by the defendant in the attachment jointlyor in common
with anotherperson,takepossessionofsuchproperty,untila bond

§ 266 [292] [Order dischargingor sustainingwhen final- appeal.]

renditionof judgment in the action,shallbe final;and can not be
reinstatedas isauthorizedby section270 ; but shallbe the subject
of appeal ifthe amount in controversybe such as to authorizean
appeal. An order sustainingan attachment shall,in likemanner ,

peal .

reinstatement of attachments as provided in section 270, shall
only be binding on the inferiorcourt untiljudgment ; and when
judgment isrendered in the actionthe inferiorcourt shallmake a
dispositionof themotion to discharge which shall,in likeman-
ner, be final.

Title IX ] [§ 317Trial- Trial by Jury .
§ 317 [347] [Orderofproceedingintrial.] When thejuryhas been
sworn , the trialshallproceed in the following order, unless the
court, for specialreasons, otherwise direct:
1. [Statement ofcasefor plaintiff.] The plaintiffmust briefly
statehis claim and the evidence by which he expects to sus-
tain it .
2. [Statement for defense.] The defendant must then briefly
state his defense and the evidence he expects to offer in sup-
port of it.
3. [Introductionof evidence.] The partyon whom reststhe
burden of proof,in the whole action,must firstproduce hisevi-
dence; the adverse party willthen produce his evidence. (Who
has burden of proof, sec. 526 ; see further as to the order of
proof, sec.592.)
4. [Evidence in rebuttal.] The partieswillthen be confined
to rebutting evidence, unless the court, for good reasons in
furtheranceof justice, permit them to offerevidencein chief.
5. [Instructions- kinds of verdict.] When the evidence is
concluded, but before the argument to the jury, eitherparty
may require the court to direct the jury to find a separate-
generalverdictwith the generalverdict[or to finda special
verdict. If a specialverdictbe so required, the questionsof
law may be reservedby the court untilafterverdict, but] if a
general verdictbe required, eitherparty may ask written in-
structionsto the jury on pointsof law, which shallbe given or
refusedby the court beforethe commencement of the argu-
ment to the jury.
6. [Order of argument.] The partiesmay then submit or
argue the caseto thejury. In theargument,thepartyhaving
the burthen of proof shallhave the conclusionand the adverse
party the opening. If therebe more than one speech on either
side,or ifseveraldefendantshaving separatedefensesappear
by differentcounsel,thecourtshallarrangetherelativeorder
of argument. (Words in bracketsstrickenout by act 1886;
seeamd .to sec.327;who has burthenof proof,sec.526;order
of proof regulatedby court, sec.592.)

(7) Abstract principlesof law. It is

tory instructionit is the duty of the
jury to make a verdictin accordance
with it. Curran v.Stein,110 Ky .99,22
R. 1575, 60 S. W. 839.
(23 ) To authorize an instructionas in
caseof a non-suit,itshouldappear that,
admitting the testimony to be true, and
every inferencethat is fairlydeducible
from it, the plaintiffhas stillfailedto
support his claim. Shay v. R. & L. Τ .
P. Co., 1 Bush 108.
(24) While it is usual for the defend-
ant,in moving fora peremptory instruc-
tion, to do so upon the plaintiff'sevi-
dence alone,yet the courtmay ,afterall
the evidence has been heard upon both
sides,directthe jury to findfor thede-
fendant, if all the evidence is in his
favor. Wilsey v. L. & N. R. R. Co., 83
Ky .511, 7 R. 498.
(25 ) If the court overrules a motion
for peremptory instructionsat conclusion
of plaintiff'stestimony, and afterward
becomes satisfiedthat this was error,
itmay , upon conclusionof all the evi-
dence, set aside former rulingand in-
struct jury to find for defendant . Nance
v . Newport News R. R., 13 R. 554, 17 S.
W. 570 ; or may reserve decision on mo-
tion made at conclusion of plaintiff'sevi-
dence until all the evidence is in. Owens
v .Meredith , 25 R. 1485, 78 S. W. 145.
(26) The rule is, "that a party may
move upon the evidenceof his antago-
nist, assuming that evidenceand every
factwhich itconducesto prove as true,
and ask the court for a peremptory in-
struction;,but he can not make such a
motion on his own evidencealone, or on
his evidencemingled with, or added to,
that of the oppositeparty, unless his
own evidenceis composed of record, or
uncontested unofficial documents or writ-
ings." United Shakers v.Underwood , 11
Bush 265 .
(27) Prominent facts. Instructions
shouldnot be givenwhich givepromi-
nence to certainportionsof theevidence,
when the whole of it should have been
leftto be consideredand weighed by the
jury. Flood v. Pragoff, 79 Ky . 607, 3
R. 372; Stokes v. Shippen, 13 Bush 180.(28 ) Written instructions. Counsel
can not complain of the action of the
court in refusing an instructionunless it
was offered in writing. Bell v. Louis-
villeR. Co., 148 Ky . 189 ; 146 S. W. 383.
(29) Jury should obey instructions
and if they do not it willbe reversible
error. Lynch v. Snead Iron Works , 116
S. W. 693, 132 Ky . 241.
(30) On a retrialafter a reversal the
instructionsgiven on the former trial
except so far as they have been criti-
cisedor condemned by the Court of Ap-
peals shouldbe given. Wall v.Demint ,
141 Ky . 515, 133 S. W. 768.
(31) Uncontradicted issue. Where a
material issue is admitted the court
may instructthe jury on itas a matter

§ 322-5] [Title IXTrial- Trialby Jury.
§ 322 [352] [Dischargeofjury- causesfor.] The jurymay be dis-
charged by the court on account of the sicknessof a juror, or
otheraccidentor calamityrequiringtheirdischarge;or,by con-
sent of both parties, or, afterthey have been kept together until
it satisfactorilyappears that there is no probabilityof their
agreeing .
§ 323 [353] [Retrialofcasesinwhichjurydischarged.] Cases in
which the jury are discharged without making a verdictshall be
triedagain at such time as the court may direct.
§ 324 [354] [Verdict- mannerofrendering- pollofjury.] When the
jury have agreed upon theirverdict, their names shallbe called
by the clerkand the verdictbe rendered by theirforeman . When
the verdictis announced ,eitherparty may requirethe jury to be
polled- which is done by the clerk or court asking each juror if
it is his verdict. If any one answers in the negative, the jury
must again be sent out for further deliberation.
§ 325 [355] [Verdictto be writtenand signed- clerkto read.] The
verdictshallbe written,signed by the foreman , and read by the
clerkto the jury,and the inquirymade ,whether or not itistheir
verdict. If any juror disagree, the jury must be sent out for
furtherdeliberation; but if no disagreementbe expressed, and
neitherpartyrequirethejuryto be polled,theverdictiscomplete
and the jury discharged from the case. (Provision when three-
fourths of jury render, sec. 2268,Ky . Stats.)
§ 325. (1) Verdict- sufficiencyof. The
provisionof the Code that the verdict
shallbe signedby the foreman ismerely
directory, and a motion to set aside a
verdictbecause not signed comes too
lateafterthejuryhas beendischarged.

§ 326. (1) Verdict- defectsin plead-
ingcuredby. Where thereisany defect,

allproof of the value of such labor, to
finda verdictfor the priceof the work
done as chargedin the petition. Baum
v. Winston, 3 Met . 127.

terywhere the defendantfailsto answer,
the jury may properly, withoutproof,rendera verdictfor theplaintifffor
more than nominal damages . Rogers v.

fiedsum,thecourtcouldnotrenderjudg-
ment fortheamountclaimedupon a
verdictof "we, the jury, find for the
plaintiff." C., O. & S. W. R. R. v. Mad-
dox, 19 R. 966,42 S.W. 1124; L. & N.
R. R. v.Hartwell,99 Ky .436,18 R. 745,
36 S. W. 183; 38 S. W. 1041.
(8) Joint defendants in tort- verdict
as to one good although there is no find-
ing as to other. C. & O. R. Co. v . Booth ,
149 Ky . 245 .

§ 331. Actions in tort- effectof trialby court.

§ 331 [361] [Actionsintort- effectoftrialby court.] 1.[Trialby

assessmentsof value,damage or amount of recoveryas itwould

had. (See furtheras to trialby court, secs. 312, 346. Form
of capias ad satisfaciendum, page 711.)
Met . 499; Bates v. Buchanan, 2 Bush

eral articlesin controversythe verdict

3 Mon. 60, 6 Mon. 52 ; and see further,

Dupree,82 Ky .678,6 R. 702; Harperv.
Harper,10Bush 447; Beelerv.Sandidge,

§ 331. (1) Court, trialby- motion

fey, 80 Ky . 176, 3 R. 677; Henderson v.
(3) Judgment - how regarded. When

whom judgment was given, and every

Title IX ] [§ 332Trial- Trial by the Court .
§ 332 [362] [Law and factsto be separatelystated, ifrequested.]
Upon trialsof questions of fact by the court, it shall not be
necessary for the court to stateitsfinding, except, generally, for
the plaintiffor defendant, unless one of the partiesrequest it,
with the view of excepting to the decisionof the court upon the
questions of law involved in the trial; in which case the court
shallstatein writingthe conclusionsof factfound, separately
from theconclusionsoflaw. (Form ofjudgment,page 722.)
man v . Meade , 13 Bush 358; and see
note 1, sec, 10.
(4) The submission of the law and
facts clothes the court with all the
rightsand functionsof a jury in de-
terminingthe facts, and in the assess-ment of damages ; and the judgment
will not be disturbedupon the ground
that it is againstthe evidencefor any
less reason than would authorize the
settingasideof theverdictof a jury.Baum v. Winston , 3 Met . 127; Mulhol-
land v . Samuels , 8 Bush 63 ; Hall v .
Roberts , 24 R. 2362, 74 S. W. 199.
(5 ) See notes 1-5, inclusive, to section
126 and sec. 330 and notes as to as-
sessment of value and damages by the
court .
(6) Submission of law and facts to
court- motion for new trial- separa-
tion of law and facts- practice. See
The Albin Co. v. Ellinger, 103 Ky . 240,
19 R. 1886, 74 S. W. 655; Beelerv. San-
didge,20 R. 1581,49 S. W. 533; Day v.
Adams ,20 R. 1827, 50 S. W. 2; and sec-

submittingthe factsto the court; or it

that the courtheard testimonyand pro-
nounced judgment thereon; but such in-
ference can not be indulged in where
nothing appears but a judgment by de-

Itwas heldthatwherea partyappeared
and filedan insufficientanswer , the fail-
ure to pleadfurtherwas not a waiverof his rightto a trialby jury. Burgess
v. Jacobs,14 B. M. 517. But the trans-
fer of a case by consentto the equity
docket is a waiver of trialby jury.
Smith v.Moberly, 15 B. M. 70.(8) Weight given to judgment. Where
the law and facts are submitted to the
court, its findingsof facts are to be

structedjury, and willnot be disturbed

Bell v.Wood , 87 Ky .56, 9 R. 917, 7 S.

§ 332. (1) Statingseparatelylaw andfacts- practice. The sectionof the Code
requiringthe court to state separately
the law and facts does not relate to the
trialof provisionalremedies. Francis
v. Burnett,84 Ky .23,7 R. 715; Haynes

S. W. 993 .
(2) When the court upon request
statesseparatelyitsconclusionsof law
and fact, theremust be an exceptionto
the conclusions of law ; a motion for a

tion will not cure omission. American
Aid Societyv. Bronger, 91 Ky . 406, 12
R. 971, 15 S.W. 1118; Beelerv.Sandidge,
20 R. 1581, 49 S. W. 533.
(3) Request for separation of law
and facts need not be made until after
judgment is renderedbut must be made
within three days thereafter. Albin v.
Ellinger, 103 Ky . 240, 19 R. 1886, 44 S.
W. 655.
(4) When an ordinaryactionis trans-
ferred to equity and heard, or where
only legalquestionsare presented, sep-
arationof law and factsis not neces-
sary. Com . v. Railroads, 95 Ky . 60, 15
R. 449, 23 S. W. 868; 56 S. W. 993;
Keaton v. Sublett, 109 Ky . 106, 22 R.
631, 58 S. W. 528.
(5) When neither the law nor the
facts, nor both combined, authorizethe
judgment, no separationis necessary.

S.W. 124; norisitnecessarytoseparate
conclusionsof law and fact in an equity

531 , 10 R. 573, 9 S. W. 720 ; Wade v .

List,82 S. W. 446,26 R. 691; nor when
an agreed state of facts is submitted to
the court. City of Owensboro v. Weir ,
95 Ky . 158, 15 R. 506, 24 S. W. 115;

§ 333 [363] [Exception- how taken- objectionwhen necessary.]
1. [Exceptiondefined.] An exceptionisan objectiontaken to a
decision of the court upon a matter of law .
2. [Exception withoutobjection.] A partymay , without a
previousobjection,exceptto a decisionagainsthim, unlessit
be made at the instanceof the adverse party.
3. [Objectionand exception, when necessary. But a party
cannot except to a decisionmade at the instanceof the adverse
party, unlessobjectionshallhave been made to the motion,
offeror requestof the adverseparty. (Exceptionsto deposi-
tions,how taken,secs.586,587.)
Cin. R. Co. v. Hansford, 100 S. W. 251,
30 R. 1105 .

§ 333. (1) Amendment . An exception
taken to the filingof an amendment
that is allowed to be filedwithout ob-
jectionisnot available; objectionshould
have been made to the filing. Helburn
v.Mofford, 7 Bush 169.
(2) Demurrer. An error in overruling
a demurrer must be exceptedto at the
time. Beaven v. Phillips, 83 Ky . 88, 7
R. 9 .
(3) Depositions. When an exception
goes to the whole of a deposition, and
some of it is competent, it should be
overruled. L.& N. R.R.Co.v.Graves,
78 Ky . 74; see further, section586.
(4) When depositionsare exceptedto,
itmust appear that the exceptionwas
acted on, or it willnot avail. Corn v.
Sims, 3 Met . 391; Lewis v. Wright, 3
Bush ,311; Walrath v.Viley,1 Bush 266.
(5) Evidence. A partymust objecttothe introductionof incompetent evi-
dence,and, ifitisallowedoverhisob-
jection,must exceptto itat the time, orthe error is waived. Crabb v. Larkin,
9 Bush 154; Helburn v. Mofford, 7 Bush
169;Russellv.Marks, 3 Met.37; L. &
N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky . 74, 1
Met . 450; where an objectionwas en-
tered to the admissionof evidence, and
at the close of the evidence the bill of
exceptionsstated"to allof which the
defendant excepted," held sufficientex-
ception. L. & N. R. R. v. Ritter, 85
Ky .368,9.R. 22, 3 S. W. 591.
(6) Finalorders. Final judgments,
whether upon issuesof law or fact,need
notbe objectedor exceptedto. Where
a demurrer to an answer is sustained
and judgment renderedagainst the de-
fendant,or where a peremptory instruc-
tionisgiven,no exceptionisnecessary.
Coffman v. Wilson, 2 Met . 542; Cray-
craft v. Duncan , 6 R. 651; Loving v .
Warren Co., 14 Bush 316; Meacham v.
L. N. R., 20 R. 112 , 45 S. W. 363. Nor
isitnecessaryto exceptto a void order.
Smith v.Blakeman , 8 Bush 476.

given an exceptionmust be taken at

ception; neitherof itselfissufficient. L.

to the decision; but where the court

necessary when decision is made at in-
stanceof adverseparty. Moss Tie Co.

Shaw , 144 Ky . 458, 139 S. W. 779.

742 .

§ 335 [365] [Billofexceptions- how prepared.] 1. [How billpre-
pared.] No particularform of exception,or billof exceptions,is
required. A partymust not,exceptas hereinafterprovided,state

order of court, that itwas filedin time,

tionsdoes not operateto stay proceed-
ings on the judgment. Wright v.Wool-
folk, 14 Bush 308.

tended in vacation. Forsythe v. Huey ,

128 S. W. 341,7 Bush 680 .

83 , 127 S. W. 523 .

(37) Motion for new trial- purpose
